what_it_does: This file is a structured extraction of a reusable product-pitch design language for ConceptV Virtual Tours, derived from 11 slides across four market verticals and three deck-depth compressions; it records visual grammar, component recipes, attention flow, content budgets, and generator rules.
observations: The file organizes findings into a taxonomy of categories: gradient-shading-opacity, geometry, type-hierarchy, padding-alignment, color-palette, atom, line-connector, texture, chrome-mode, zone, sequence, and attention-direction.
Each finding includes a name, recipe, evidence slide reference, emergent boolean, and a generator_rule meant to drive future production.

A component library is derived from the findings, with named reusable units such as ChevronProcessBar, SplitHeroBlock, StageSection, PhotoRailStack, StatProofBox, ComparisonTable, ClosingCTAZone, WhatNextStub, and TestimonialBox.
Content samples are annotated with character, word, and line budgets for specific slots like hero headlines, section heads, body paragraphs, bullets, and italic promise lines.
The file explicitly notes that property and residential decks are observed-identical templates, while architects and gatehouse follow different audience-variant grammars.
There are no code symbols in the file; symbols[] is empty.
standouts: A single amber/goldenrod accent (#E0A82C) is assigned the unified semantic load of YES / HERE / NOW / ASPIRE across every active state, arrow, checkmark, headline, border, and CTA.
The same narrative grammar is said to scale invariantly across 4-page, 3-page, and 2-page deck depths by compressing or stubbing journey stages.
Audience intent drives layout grammar: architects receive a comparison table and stat-proof zone, while gatehouse/property/residential receive a chevron journey bar with photo right-rail.
Headline color is treated as a semantic register: amber for aspirational/emotional sections, near-black for functional/rational sections.
conventions: Follows a consistent JSON schema with named categories, evidence references, and generator rules.
Uses approximate CSS-style values (rgba, hex, px) for colors, spacing, and shadows.
Distinguishes emergent patterns from explicit ones with an emergent boolean.
Provides content-budget constraints (character and word counts) for most textual slots.
Maintains a neutral, descriptive tone throughout, avoiding prescriptive ranking language.
concerns: The content_samples section is truncated mid-entry (chevron-label shows "chars": 1 with a truncation note), leaving the slot specification incomplete.
Color and spacing values are approximate and lack a documented sampling or measurement method.
The claim that property and residential decks are observed-identical is asserted without embedded image hashes or side-by-side evidence.
Some attention_flow entries blend inferred eye movement with factual layout description, which may be interpretive rather than observed.
notes: A dense design-system audit that tries to turn a set of pitch decks into a generative grammar with audience and depth parameters.
questions: What source images or slide files were used to derive the exact color and spacing values?
How was the emergent flag assigned for each finding?
Is the property/residential identity literal pixel-match or functional similarity?
What tool or process produced this extraction, and has any generator rule been validated by re-rendering?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file presents itself as an extracted design substrate and delivers a structured design-language document with reusable components and generator rules.
apparent_themes: Order as meaning: hook, proof, possibility, close
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to convert a manual design audit into a reproducible, parameterizable system for generating consistent product pitch decks across audiences and lengths.
novelty: It treats deck length as a compression ratio and audience type as a grammar selector, pairing visual rules with explicit content budgets for each textual slot.
contribution: It contributes a taxonomy of design findings, generator rules with content budgets, a named component library with parameters, and a compression/audience-variant grammar that a fused system could reuse as a structured pitch-deck design substrate.
summary_for_embedding: A structured extraction of the ConceptV Virtual Tours pitch-deck design language across four verticals and three depth compressions, including visual grammar, components, attention flow, and generative rules.
intention_for_embedding: To codify the observed pitch-deck design patterns into a reusable, parameterizable system for producing audience-specific and depth-compressed product decks.
